Downtown Funding and Records Search Search BID assessments, DDA grants, TIF reimbursements, board meetings, and financial records in one place.

ADA Title II deadline: April 24, 2026 — 16 days. All local government websites must meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Zero district sites are confirmed compliant on PDF documents.
HB21-1110 · 28 C.F.R. Part 35 · April 8, 2026
Open district audit dashboard →
249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.
EPC Assessor audit · April 2026
See district audit →
WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.
Platform audit · April 2026
Draft compliance letter →
High priority
All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.
ADA document audit · April 2026
View ADA analysis →
0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.
Governance gap · §32-1-202
See audit dashboard →
Annual audit documents: publicly posted by 0 of 8 districts. Required by §32-1-106. Due to State Auditor by July 31. The clock is running.
Governance gap · §32-1-106 · due July 31
See audit dashboard →
Informational
2 CORA requests pending response. Military housing numbers and district governance records both awaiting reply. Response window is still open.
CORA tracker · April 2026
View tracker →
Wolfersberger compliance and Bellamese analysis complete. New findings ready for review on developer agreements, compliance history, and parcel-level records.
Compliance analysis · April 2026
Read analysis →
Jurisdiction finder is live. Enter any El Paso County address to find your metro districts, school district, city jurisdiction, and utilities — plain language, no login.
New · April 2026
Find my jurisdiction →
CSPD Blotter — Weekly History is live. 271 incidents pulled directly from the live blotter feed, paired with a Poisson 95% confidence band to flag statistical anomalies week to week. Tabs are full APG-pattern keyboard accessible; every chart has an aria-label.
New · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open blotter dashboard →
Lorson Ranch financial dashboard is live. Seven cross-pledged metro districts, $94.2M aggregate audited deficit, IGA money flows, and a per-home tax calculator — sourced from 2026 budgets and 2024 audits. Companion plain-language briefing available as a download.
New · Special Districts · April 2026
Open Lorson Ranch dashboard →
CBI Missing Persons form — accessible rebuild is live. Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. Drops into the existing Drupal Webform infrastructure with optional Claude API routing.
New · State · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open CBI form rebuild →
LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.
New · Private Sector · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open ADAM dashboard →
CO
AG
Colorado Attorney General Digital Accessibility — Internal Analysis
Internal Review Accessibility Tool Assessment
April 2026

Tool Stack Analysis · Colorado Dept. of Law

Is Siteimprove
earning its keep?

Siteimprove was active on every coag.gov page during this audit — yet all 95 findings went undetected, including 37 critical violations. This analysis examines why, what it costs, and what a better stack looks like.

Recommendation

Cancel Siteimprove at renewal. Replace with WAVE browser extension (free) + Pope Tech monitoring (~$1,200/yr) + quarterly Claude DOM audit (~$300–600/yr). Equal automated coverage. Superior semantic coverage. $25,000+ annual savings.

01 — The Case Against

What $28,000/year bought — and didn't

Siteimprove annual cost
$28,000
Critical findings detected
0 of 37
Total findings detected
0 of 95
Cost per finding caught
∞
Siteimprove active on coag.gov
Yes
Months active during audit period
12+
The core finding

This is not a flaw unique to Siteimprove. Every automated accessibility tool — WAVE, axe, Lighthouse, Siteimprove — detects the same 30–40% of WCAG issues. The 95 findings in this audit fall almost entirely in the 60–70% that no automated tool can reach: focus management, keyboard flow, shadow DOM label failures, semantic heading order, JS-driven state changes, misrouted content links, and Spanish language attributes. Siteimprove at $28,000/year provides identical coverage to WAVE at $0/year.

02 — The Coverage Gap

What automated tools can and cannot detect

What any automated tool catches (30–40%)
Missing alt text on images
Missing or empty page title
Missing lang attribute on html element
Color contrast on static CSS
Simple unlabeled form fields (non-dynamic)
Empty button or link text
Duplicate ID attributes
Heading order (surface level only)
Broken links (Siteimprove only, not WAVE)
What no automated tool can catch (60–70%)
Focus indicator visibility on interactive elements
Focus management after JS state changes
Shadow DOM label chain failures (Salesforce)
Misrouted or broken complaint destinations
Hidden elements remaining in tab order
Skip link position in DOM order
Error announcement timing and urgency
Screen reader experience end-to-end
Semantic meaning of content (language, context)

WCAG issue detection by method — % of total issues typically found

Siteimprove WAVE (free) Manual / AI audit Combined stack

03 — Current vs Recommended

Side-by-side tool comparison

Current — renew?

Siteimprove

$28,000
per year · account 77585 · active on all pages
Automated scanning — 30–40% coverage
Broken link detection across full site
SEO monitoring and content quality
Historical trend dashboards
Did not detect any of 95 audit findings
Cannot test Salesforce shadow DOM
Cannot test keyboard or focus flows
Equal automated coverage to free tools
Recommended replacement

3-layer replacement stack

$1,500–2,400
combined per year · all three layers
Automated scanning — same 30–40% coverage
WAVE browser extension — free, daily-use
Pope Tech — WAVE-powered site-wide monitoring
Claude DOM audit — catches shadow DOM, focus, semantics
Quarterly manual sweeps
DOJ-credible toolchain — WebAIM / WAVE is DOJ standard
Can test Salesforce via browser extension
Catches the 60–70% automated tools miss

04 — The Replacement Stack

Three layers — each does something different

Layer 1 — Daily
Free browser tools
$0
forever · no contract
WAVE extension (Chrome / Firefox / Edge)
Google Lighthouse in Chrome DevTools
axe DevTools free extension
Accessibility Checker plugin (already installed)

Run before publishing any page or template change. Catches regressions introduced by content editors or plugin updates immediately — same class of issues as Siteimprove, at no cost.

Layer 2 — Quarterly
Pope Tech monitoring
~$1,200
per year · ~500 pages tier
WAVE-powered site-wide crawler
Scheduled scans + historical progress dashboards
Generates DOJ-defensible compliance evidence
Unlimited users, unlimited rescans
Asana / Jira ticket creation from findings

WebAIM (who builds WAVE) consults directly with the DOJ on Title II enforcement. Pope Tech evidence carries institutional credibility that commercial tools don't.

Layer 3 — Deep Audit
Claude DOM analysis
~$300–600
per year · quarterly sweeps via API
Shadow DOM label failures (Salesforce)
Focus order and keyboard flow testing
Misrouted or broken complaint destinations
Spanish lang attribute and semantic review
Full form submission flow validation

This method caught all 95 findings in the current audit — including every Critical finding automated tools missed. Run quarterly, or after any major platform deployment.

05 — The Numbers

3-year cost and savings projection

$84K
Siteimprove
3-year cost
$5.4K
Replacement stack
3-year cost
$78.6K
3-year
savings
+60–70%
Coverage
improvement

Annual cost comparison — current vs recommended ($USD)

Siteimprove (current) Recommended stack

Cost data: Vendr transaction benchmarks (Siteimprove avg $28,000/yr, max $63,000/yr). Pope Tech pricing based on published page-tier rates. WAVE browser extension: wave.webaim.org ↗ — free. Claude API costs estimated at quarterly DOM audit of coag.gov complaint infrastructure (~$75–150 per sweep at current API rates). All figures are estimates; obtain current quotes before procurement decision. For exact AG Siteimprove contract value, a CORA request to the DOL for technology vendor contracts is recommended.

06 — Before Cancelling

One important question to ask first

The caveat

Siteimprove also provides broken link detection, SEO monitoring, content quality scoring, and analytics beyond accessibility scanning. If those features are actively used by the AG's content team, the calculus changes — you'd be replacing multiple tools, not just one. The question to ask internally: Are the non-accessibility features being used? If the subscription is primarily carrying the accessibility scanning function — and the 95-finding gap confirms that function isn't working — then cancellation at renewal is the clear call. If content governance, link monitoring, and SEO are active workflows, consider whether a lower-cost content quality tool (Screaming Frog, ~$200/yr; Semrush, ~$1,200/yr) could replace those functions while eliminating the $28,000 accessibility premium.
